aliases: Beef Burger with Cheese and Salad

english: Beef Burger with Cheese and Salad

ontology.foodex2_description: Hamburger / Burger — composite meat sandwich in a bun

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one burger set comprising the beef and cheese burger (approximately 200 g), a side mesclun salad (approximately 50 g), and a portion of fries (approximately 100 g), totalling around 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Beef & Cheese Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef & Cheese Burger (cr_id G00010453). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 21110 — Beef patty, cooked, well done) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish; value cross-checked against USDA composite burger entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on white hamburger bun (GI ~61–72) and composite burger meal
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Beef & Cheese Burger. GI estimated at 66 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle (white wheat hamburger bun, GI ~61–72, University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread/bun analogues) and the moderating effect of protein (13.3 g/100g) and fat (10.18 g/100g) on gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 29.57 g/100g = 103.5 g) ÷ 100 = 68.3; however, the burger component alone (200 g) yields GL = 66 × 59.14 ÷ 100 = 39.0. Combined set GL reported as 54.3 using weighted carbohydrate across the full 350 g set (82.2 g available carbohydrate × 66 ÷ 100 = 54.3). This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this dish has not been performed.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g with insufficient potassium (197 mg/100g) and fibre (1.8 g/100g) offset
hypertension.guidance: A standard 200 g burger portion delivers approximately 974 mg sodium — nearly the full daily DASH diet sodium limit in a single meal — and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio (0.40) provides no meaningful cardiovascular offset; individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption and request reduced condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (29.57 g/100g) from white bun with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) producing elevated glycaemic response; estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100g) worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined white bun contributes a medium-to-high glycaemic index (~66) with minimal fibre buffering, and the estimated trans fat content worsens insulin sensitivity; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute a wholegrain bun or lettuce wrap, limit portion to half a serving, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.8 g/100g) and estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100g) from beef patty and processed cheese, combined with high sodium (487 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from beef and processed cheese, estimated trans fat, and high sodium creates a triple cardiovascular risk burden; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, choose leaner protein alternatives, and request no added condiments to reduce sodium and atherogenic fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from red beef amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; estimated trans fat (~0.3 g/100g) strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which directly amplifies RA inflammatory cascades, and the estimated trans fat content is a recognised pro-inflammatory trigger; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute grilled fish or chicken and avoid regular consumption of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Beef and Cheese Burger is a Western-style composite dish served in Singapore cafés and casual dining restaurants. It consists of a toasted garlic wheat bun enclosing a homemade grilled beef patty, a slice of processed cheese, fresh lettuce, and sliced tomato, accompanied by a side mesclun salad and a portion of fries. The burger delivers a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a juicy, slightly charred patty, melted creamy cheese, and a soft toasted bun with garlic butter notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium and 116 mg calcium. The refined white bun creates a medium-to-high glycaemic load (estimated GI ~66), and the low dietary fibre content (1.8 g/100g) provides minimal glycaemic buffering. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension (sodium 487 mg/100g, potassium only 197 mg/100g), Type 2 Diabetes (refined carbohydrate, low fibre, estimated trans fat worsening insulin resistance), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from beef and cheese, estimated trans fat, high sodium). The dish is not suitable for individuals managing any of these conditions without significant modification. It is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner and is classified under grains and staples, bread-based dishes. The nutrient data is sourced from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010453, confidence 0.95).
common_user_queries: is beef burger healthy for diabetes

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010453, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy for a comparable beef burger composite and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21110, beef patty cooked) and USDA composite burger data, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 2. Trans fat (0.3 g/100g) is an estimate based on known trans fat content of beef (natural ruminant trans fat ~0.2–0.5 g/100g of beef) and processed cheese; not directly reported by HPB. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Singapore burger dish are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central composite burger entries as of 2026. This data is unavailable from approved sources for this specific composite dish. 4. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on white hamburger bun GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, AJCN); no direct GI measurement for this specific dish has been published. 5. The dish is served as a set meal (burger + mesclun salad + fries); per-100g values reflect the composite dish as reported by HPB and may include the side components in the weighted average. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains beef and the HPB record does not specify halal certification.
